coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of Zn2+ nanocarriers from Auricularia auricula fermentation broth with excellent antioxidant activity
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Xiaoting
Xing, Shanghua
Tan, Mingqian
coredata.dc:description: Nanoparticles (NPs) possessing nanoscale dimensions and remarkable antioxidant activity were synthesized via a green hydrothermal method utilizing Auricularia auricula fermentation broth, referred to as AFNPs. The functional groups on the surface of the AFNPs significantly contributed to the formation of AFNPs-Zn2+. The AFNPs-Zn2+ appeared a zinc retention rate of 40.80 % after gastrointestinal digestion. When compared to typical zinc supplements, AFNPs-Zn2+ did not exhibit visible cytotoxicity or hemolysis. Furthermore, AFNPs-Zn2+ demonstrated the ability to mitigate cell damage resulting from zinc deficiency. In vivo experiments showed that AFNPs-Zn2+ were mainly observed in the stomach, intestine, kidney, and testis after oral administration. In vivo distribution experiments indicated predominant presence of AFNPs-Zn2+ in the stomach, intestine, kidney, and testis following oral administration. This study highlights the potential for Auricularia auricula NPs to serve as the efficient, stable, and safe nanocarriers for Zn2+.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological feature analysis of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+. (a) The preparation, formation mechanism, and activity evaluation of AFNPs-Zn2+. (b) TEM image of AFNPs synthesized by FB-3, and the insets were the histogram of particle size distribution and the visualized pictures corresponding to AFNPs, respectively. (c) The zinc-binding rate of AFNPs synthesized by FB-0, FB-1, and FB-3, respectively. (d) TEM image of AFNPs-Zn2+, and the inset was the visualized pictures of AFNPS-Zn2+. (e) The hydrodynamic diameter of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ by DLS measurement.

Optical and structural characteristics analysis of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+. The fluorescence emission spectra of (a) AFNPs and (b) AFNPs-Zn2+. Insets represent the pictures of AFNPs or AFNPs-Zn2+ aqueous solution under natural light (left) and UV light (right). (c) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+. (d) XRD, (e) FTIR, (f) XPS, and (g) 1H NMR spectra of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+. The enlarged 1H NMR spectra in the range of (h) 1.94–1.80 ppm, (i) 2.27–2.21 ppm, (j) 4.03–3.97 ppm, (k) 5.50–5.40 ppm, (l) 7.21–7.15 ppm, (m) 8.52–8.16 ppm.

Antioxidant activity analysis of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+. The ABTS scavenging ability of (a) AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+, (b) trolox (positive control). The DPPH scavenging ability of (c) AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+, (d) ascorbic acid (positive control). The hydroxyl radical scavenging ability of (e) AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+, (f) ascorbic acid (positive control). (g) The antioxidant mechanism of AFNPs-Zn2+.

Biocompatibility analysis and protective effect on TPEN damage of AFNPs-Zn2+. The cell viability of IEC-6 cells after treatment with (a) AFNPs, (b) AFNPs-Zn2+, zinc gluconate, and ZnSO4. (c) The hemolysis rate of AFNPs-Zn2+ at 1 or 3 h, respectively. (d) Fluorescence images of IEC-6 cells stained with calcein-AM (green, living cells) and PI (red, dead cells). Scale bars = 100 μm. Mean fluorescence intensity of (e) living cells, (f) dead cells. (g) Quantitative analysis of the cell viabilities of IEC-6 cells after different treatments by MTT assay. Different letters represent significant differences (p < 0.05). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cellular antioxidant enzyme activity analysis. (a) Fluorescence images and (b) mean fluorescence intensity of IEC-6 cells stained with DCFH-DA. Scale bars = 100 μm. The content of (c) total antioxidant capacity (T-AOC), (d) catalase (CAT), (e) superoxide dismutase (SOD), (f) glutathione (GSH), (g) malondialdehyde (MDA), and (h) Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) in IEC-6 cells damaged by TPEN. Different letters indicate significant differences (p < 0.05).

In vivo biodistribution of AFNPs-Zn2+. (a) Schematic diagram of in vivo distribution experiment. (b) Fluorescence images, (c) fluorescence intensity, and (d) zinc content in major organs (stomach, intestine, kidney, and testis) after oral administration with AFNPs-Zn2+ for 0.5, 2, 6, and 24 h. “0h” means the mice were executed immediately after being given the equal volume of distilled water as other experimental groups. Different letters indicate significant differences (p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of Zn2+ nanocarriers from Auricularia auricula fermentation broth with excellent antioxidant activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanoparticles (NPs) possessing nanoscale dimensions and remarkable antioxidant activity were synthesized via a green hydrothermal method utilizing Auricularia auricula fermentation broth, referred to as AFNPs. The functional groups on the surface of the AFNPs significantly contributed to the formation of AFNPs-Zn2+. The AFNPs-Zn2+ appeared a zinc retention rate of 40.80 % after gastrointestinal digestion. When compared to typical zinc supplements, AFNPs-Zn2+ did not exhibit visible cytotoxicity or hemolysis. Furthermore, AFNPs-Zn2+ demonstrated the ability to mitigate cell damage resulting from zinc deficiency. In vivo experiments showed that AFNPs-Zn2+ were mainly observed in the stomach, intestine, kidney, and testis after oral administration. In vivo distribution experiments indicated predominant presence of AFNPs-Zn2+ in the stomach, intestine, kidney, and testis following oral administration. This study highlights the potential for Auricularia auricula NPs to serve as the efficient, stable, and safe nanocarriers for Zn2+.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Zinc deficiency is a prevalent human disorder that leads to oxidative stress. This stress, in turn, affects oxidant-sensitive transcription factors, potentially resulting in various diseases (Oteiza & Mackenzie, 2005). Statistically, zinc deficiency affects a significant portion of the global population, with specific groups such as pregnant women and developing children experiencing an increasing impact. Fortunately, zinc deficiency can be prevented by the intake of zinc-containing foods (e.g., oysters, animal liver, lean meat, etc.) in the daily diet. However, the zinc content in food is relatively limited, and the dietary intake is vulnerable to the harsh environment (e.g., enzymes, phytic acid, and cellulose) in the digestive tract, which makes it difficult to meet the daily needs of the human body (Fredlund et al., 2006). Therefore, it is necessary to develop appropriate zinc supplements with excellent stability, satisfactory safety, and reliable bioavailability to prevent zinc deficiency.

Currently, zinc supplements are formulated using three types of carriers: inorganic anions, organic acids, and peptides. While these supplements have significantly addressed zinc deficiency, they each possess certain limitations. For example, zinc supplements prepared with inorganic anions and organic acids not only have a low absorption rate, but also have a strong stimulating effect on the gastrointestinal tract (Dong et al., 2018). Furthermore, although zinc supplements prepared with peptides have good bioavailability, the low zinc-binding ability and the high cost of preparation and separation largely limit their practical application (Yu et al., 2022). Therefore, it is necessary to find a safe and effective Zn2+ carrier to solve these problems. Additionally, while zinc supplements formulated with peptides exhibit good bioavailability, their practical application is significantly constrained by the peptides' low zinc-binding ability and the high cost involved in their preparation and separation (Yu et al., 2022). Consequently, there is a pressing need to discover a safe and efficient Zn2+ carrier capable of addressing these limitations.

Recently, there has been significant interest in food-borne nanoparticles (NPs) synthesized through hydrothermal methods using food as a carbon source. The formation of these NPs involves intricate physicochemical reactions during the thermal processing of food, resembling the generation of carbon dots via the top-down method (Liu et al., 2024). These NPs have many advantages, such as nanoscale size (<10 nm), good dissolvability, and excellent quantum yield. More importantly, they are enriched with multifarious functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups), outstanding penetrability, praised antioxidant activity, and superior biosecurity, giving them the possibility to be used as Zn2+ nanocarriers to alleviate cell, tissue, and organ oxidative damage caused by zinc deficiency (Qi et al., 2023). Currently, NPs have been used for the delivery of active ingredients such as curcumin, lutein, and doxorubicin (Duan, et al., 2019; Jv et al., 2023; Wen et al., 2022). However, there are relatively few studies on AFNPs in Zn2+ delivery.

The Auricularia auricula (A. auricula) fermentation broth, derived from microbial fermentation, is a nutrient-rich food abundant in various active substances, as previously demonstrated in studies (Yu et al., 2021). Herein, AFNPs with outstanding antioxidant activity were greenly synthesized by hydrothermal method by using A. auricula fermentation broth. The complex of AFPNs and Zn2+, called AFNPs-Zn2+, was prepared by one-bath method. Firstly, the zinc-binding ability of NPs was evaluated in different pH values to screen the optimal preparation scheme. The formation of AFNPs-Zn2+ was analyzed by morphological features, optical properties, and structural characteristics. Secondly, the in vitro digestive properties, in vivo distribution, and biocompatibility of AFNPs-Zn2+ were also investigated. Finally, a zinc deficiency model of intestinal epithelial cell-6 (IEC-6 cells) was constructed to evaluate the beneficial effect of AFNPs-Zn2+. The results showed that the NPs hydrothermally synthesized with A. auricula fermentation broth as a carbon source have incredible potential to be used as Zn2+ nanocarrier.

The A. auricula was acquired from Beidahuang Food Company in Qiqihaer, China. D101 macroporous resin was purchased from Yuanye Biotechnology Company in Shanghai, China. Zinc sulfate (ZnSO4) was obtained from Jiuding Chemical Technology Company in Shanghai, China. The ethanol was obtained from Damao Chemical Reagent in Tianjin, China. The chemicals of MnSO4·H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, and CaCl2·2H2O were brought from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Company in Shanghai, China. The Co (NO3)2·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, and CuCl2·2H2O were acquired from Macklin Biochemical Technology Company in Shanghai, China. The simulated saliva fluid, gastric fluid, and intestinal fluid were brought from Yuanye Biotechnology Company in Shanghai, China. The dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 300 Da was purchased from Yuanye Biotechnology Company in Shanghai, China. The intestinal epithelioid cell line No. 6 (IEC-6 cell) was bought from Fenghui Biological Company in Changsha, China. N, N, N’, N’-tetrakis (2-pyridylmethyl) ethylenediamine (TPEN) was acquired from MedChemExpress Company in Shanghai, China. Nitric acid (HNO3) was obtained from Xilong Scientific Company in Shantou, China.

In the previous study, two types of A. auricula fermentation broth, namely one strain fermentation broth (FB-1) and three strains fermentation broth (FB-3) were prepared (Yu et al., 2021). The A. auricula fermentation broth powder (1.25 g) was dissolved in distilled water (25 mL). Next, the solution was added into an autoclave reactor and hydrothermally reacted at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling, the sample solution in the reactor was centrifuged (6000 rpm, 20 min) to collect the yellow supernatant. 100 mL of ethanol was affiliated into the yellow solution, and stored in the refrigerator (4 °C) for 36 h. The liquid was separated by centrifugation (6000 rpm, 20 min) and concentrated to 10 mL. The resulting solution was added into the D101 macroporous resin column and eluted with distilled water. The eluent with fluorescence property was gathered as AFNPs, then lyophilized and stored at −20 °C for further use. Furthermore, the unfermented A. auricula water extract (FB-0) was prepared by mixing 2.5 mg of A. auricula powder into 150 mL distilled water at 22 ℃ for 4 h. The AFNPs were synthesized by using FB-0 in the same way, and compared with the AFNPs synthesized by using FB-1 and FB-3 in subsequent experiments. The preparation method of FB-0 is referred to the previous research from our group (Yu et al., 2021).

The stability of AFNPs in different metal ions, pH buffer, and NaCl solution was measured. Particularly, 200 μL of 1 mg/mL AFNPs solution was dropped into 200 μL of 1 mmol/L metal ions solution (MnSO4·H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, Co (NO3)2·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, and MgCl2·6H2O), Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution (pH = 1–11), and NaCl solution (1–10 mg/mL), respectively. The fluorescence intensity of the samples was measured at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm and emission wavelength in the range of 390–600 nm with a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, F-2700, Japan) after 20 min of reaction.

The AFNPs-Zn2+ was prepared by one-step water bath method. Specifically, 10 mg of AFNPs and 10 mg of zinc sulfate were both dissolved in 2 mL of 50 mM different pH buffer solution. The mixed liquid was reacted at 60 °C for 2 h to synthesize AFNPs-Zn2+. After the reaction finished, free Zn2+ was removed by dialysis (MW 300 Da). The AFNPs-Zn2+ in the dialysis bag was collected, and the solid powder was obtained by freeze-drying. The determination of zinc content was measured using the atomic absorption spectrophotometer (Hitachi, ZA3000, Japan) after adding 10 mL of deionization to the solid powder. The zinc-binding rate and zinc loading efficiency were computed by the formula:

where m0 was the initial input of zinc, m1 was the amount of zinc in the dialysis bag, and m2 was the amount of the initial input of AFNPs.

The morphology of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ was observed through TEM (JEOL, JEM-2100, Japan) by dropping an aqueous solution onto a carbon-coated copper grid. The hydrodynamic diameter of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ was analyzed using a dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (LS Instruments, LS-0118–0079, Switzerland). The absorbance spectra of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ were conducted using UV–vis spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, Lambda 35, USA). The fluorescence spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, F-2700, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime was conducted by a fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, FLS980, UK). The information about surface functional groups in AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ was measured by the infrared spectrometer (PerkinElmer, VECTOR 22, USA) via the KBr disc method. The crystallinity/amorphous nature of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ was obtained from XRD analysis (Shimadzu, XRD-6100, Japan). The elementary composition in the surface of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ was analyzed by XPS (Thermo, ESCALAB 250, USA). The displacement of the hydrogen proton during the formation of AFNPs-Zn2+ was analyzed using a spectrometer (Bruker, AVANCE III, Germany).

The determination of ABTS radical scavenging ability was based on the principle of electron transfer. Briefly, 1 mL of 7.4 mM ABTS liquid was mixed with 1 mL of 2.6 mM K2S2O8 liquid to produce ABTS radical cation. After storing in darkness for 12 h, the cationic liquid was diluted to an initial absorbance value of 0.7 ± 0.02 at 734 nm. ABTS operating fluid (120 μL) was reacted with 30 μL of AFNPs or AFNPs-Zn2+ (50, 100, 150, 200, and 250 μg/mL) for 6 min to determine ABTS radical scavenging ability, respectively. The scavenging ability of trolox was measured as a positive control. The ABTS radical scavenging activity was computed by equation (3) according to the absorbance at 734 nm determined with a microplate reader (Tecan, Infinite 200 Multimode, Switzerland).
(3)

A 0
× 100 %
where A0 was the absorbance of distilled water and operating fluid, A1 was the absorbance of sample fluid and operating fluid, and A2 was the absorbance of sample fluid.

The proton transfer was reacted to measure the 2,2-diphenyl-1-(2,4,6-trinitrophenyl) hydrazyl (DPPH) radical scavenging ability of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+. Concretely, 100 μL of 0.01 mM DPPH ethanol operating fluid was reacted with 100 μL AFNPs or AFNPs-Zn2+ (20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 μg/mL) for 30 min to determine the DPPH radical scavenging ability, respectively (Huang et al., 2018). The radical scavenging of ascorbic acid was measured as a positive control. The scavenging activity of DPPH radical was determined by equation (3) on the basis of the absorbance at 517 nm.

As for the hydroxyl radical, 1 mL of AFNPs or AFNPs-Zn2+ (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5 mg/mL) and 1 mL of each operating liquid (9 mM FeSO4, 9 mM salicylic acid, and 2.4 mM H2O2) were mixed in sequence at 37 °C (water-bath) to investigate the scavenging ability of hydroxyl radical. After reacting for 30 min, the scavenging activity was measured on the basis of the absorbance at 510 nm by equation (3) (Souza et al., 2012). Synchronously, the radical scavenging of ascorbic acid was measured as a positive control.

In vitro digestion behavior of AFNPs-Zn2+ was investigated according to the oral-gastro-intestinal digestion method. Firstly, AFNPs-Zn2+ solution (1 mL, 1 mg/mL) was reacted with 2 mL simulated saliva fluid (pH = 6.5) at 37 °C for 5 min. Next, simulated gastric fluid (2 mL) was added and incubated at pH = 2 (adjusted by 1 mol/L HCl solution) for 2 h. Finally, simulated intestinal fluid (2 mL) was added and reacted at pH = 8 (adjusted by 2 mol/L NaOH solution) for another 2 h. The fluorescence intensity of the digestion fluid in each reaction was determined after centrifugating at 6000 rpm for 10 min. The digestive fluids were substituted with distilled water in the control group. Fluorescence quenching efficiency was considered to be the ratio of fluorescence intensity between the AFNPs-Zn2+ and control group. The reaction solution after digestion of gastric juice and intestinal juice was dialyzed through a 300 Da dialysis bag for 12 h. The determination of zinc content in the dialysis bag was investigated by the atomic absorption spectrophotometer to calculate the retention rate of AFNPs-Zn2+.

The cytotoxicity of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ was determined by the MTT assay. The IEC-6 cells were inoculated in 96-well plates (1 × 105 cells/well) and treated with AFNPs (0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 1, and 2 mg/mL) or AFNPs-Zn2+ liquid (zinc content of 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 μg/mL) for 24 h, respectively. Meanwhile, the cells were treated with zinc sulfate (ZnSO4) and zinc gluconate at the same zinc concentration for comparison. Next, 20 μL of MTT (5 mg/mL) liquid was affiliated and incubated for another 3 h. Subsequently, the supernate was replaced with 150 μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). After shaking for 20 min, the cell viability was computed according to the absorbance at 490 nm by the following formula:
(4)
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
%
=
A 1
A 0
× 100 %
where A0 was the absorbance of the cells with no treatment, A1 was the absorbance of the cells after treatment with AFNPs, AFNPs-Zn2+, ZnSO4, and zinc gluconate.

In brief, the blood from the BALB/c mouse was used to determine the hemolysis rate. 500 μL of blood was rinsed with 10 mL of phosphate buffered solution (PBS, pH 7.4) for 3 times. After centrifugation (10000 rpm, 5 min), the red blood cells on the bottom were re-dissolved in PBS (10 mL). Then, 200 μL of red blood cell suspension was reacted with 800 μL of AFNPs-Zn2+ liquid (1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 mg/mL) at 37 °C for 1 h or 3 h, respectively. Meanwhile, the blood cells treated with distilled water and PBS were regarded as the positive and negative control, respectively. After centrifugation (10000 rpm) for 5 min, the hemolysis rate was computed according to the absorbance at 570 nm by the formula:
(5)

× 100 %
where A0, A1, A2 were the absorbance of the positive, negative and AFNPs-Zn2+ control, respectively.

IEC-6 cells were inoculated in 12-well plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells/pore. 1 mL of fresh medium containing AFNPs-Zn2+ with different concentrations (30, 50, 50 μg/mL) was incubated for 24 h, respectively. After removing the original medium,1 mL of medium containing 6 μM TPEN was added into the 12-well plates and stimulated for another 2 h. As for the determination of intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS), cells were exposed to 0.5 mL of PBS buffer solution containing DCFH-DA (10 μg/mL) for 30 min. As for live/dead assay, the IEC-6 cells were stained by calcein-AM and prodium iodide (PI). The fluorescence of the cells was examined by a fluorescence inverted microscope (Nikon, Ti-S, Japan) and quantitated by Image J software after washing thrice with PBS. As for the content of cellular antioxidant enzyme activity, including total antioxidant capacity (T-AOC), catalase (CAT), malondialdehyde (MDA), superoxide dismutase (SOD), glutathione (GSH), and Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) were detected according to the biochemical kit from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Company in Nanjing, China.

The male BALB/c mice (20 ± 0.5 g) were obtained from Changsheng Biotechnology Company in Shenyang, China. All animal procedures were performed in accordance with protocols approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Dalian Polytechnic University. According to the different retention time of AFNPs-Zn2+ in the body, the mice were divided into 5 groups (n = 3), including 0, 0.5, 2, 6, and 24 h group. After acclimatization for 7 days, the mice were executed at 0.5, 2, 6, and 24 h, respectively, after oral administration with 50 mg/kg of AFNPs-Zn2+. The mice in control group (0 h group) were executed immediately after oral administration with the equal volume of distilled water. The fluorescence distribution in various organs was detected by a Multi-functional in vivo imaging system (Molecular Device, MIIS, USA). After imaging, the organs were put into the digestive tube. 10 mL of nitric acid was added to digest for 6 h. Until all organs were completely dissolved, the digestive tube was heated at 160 °C on an electric heating furnace for 1.5 h to volatilize the nitric acid. Finally, the remaining solution was volume constant with deionized water to 50 mL to calculate the zinc content in organs.
The data in this research were the average value of 3 parallel experiments and were analyzed by using SPSS 19.0 software. The diagrams were drafted by Origin 2018 software.

In this research, AFNPs prepared by the green synthesis method with A. auricula fermentation broth were used as nanocarriers of Zn2+ in Fig. 1 a. The AFNPs were caramel-colored powders (the inset of Fig. 1b), and the color of AFNPs-Zn2+ became lighter than AFNPs (the inset of Fig. 1d). TEM images (Fig. 1b and S1) showed that AFNPs were subspherical particles with uniform size, which had the general morphological characteristics of NPs synthesized by the hydrothermal method (Sun et al., 2013). The formation of these AFNPs was related to the physicochemical changes (such as carbonylation and caramelization) of nutrients in A. auricula fermentation broth (Wang et al., 2020). Under high temperature and pressure, the nutrients such as polysaccharides, proteins, polyphenols, and melanins in A. auricula fermentation broth were broken down and concentrated into AFNPs. Importantly, they all had nano-scale sizes, with average particle sizes of 2.7, 2.6, and 1.9 nm, respectively. It meant that AFNPs had the unique advantage to be Zn2+ nanocarriers, which were greater permeability into various biological barriers (cell membranes, blood–brain barrier) (Bi et al., 2018). Meanwhile, the zinc-binding ability of AFNPs was evaluated at different pH values. As shown in Fig. 1c, with the increase of pH value, the zinc-binding rate increased first and then decreased, reaching a maximum at near-neutral conditions. It was the result of the competition of Zn2+ for the electron-donating groups with hydrogen ions in an acidic solution and the formation of hydroxide precipitate in an alkaline solution (Qiao et al., 2021). Notably, AFNPs synthesized with FB-3 had the strongest binding ability to Zn2+, followed by FB-1 and FB-0, with the maximum zinc-binding rate of 31.22 % (pH = 8), 27.24 % (pH = 9), and 21.42 % (pH = 8), respectively. Therefore, AFNPs synthesized with FB-3 were selected to prepare a large amount of AFNPs-Zn2+ at pH = 8 in the following experiment. The zinc loading efficiency of AFNPs-Zn2+ was calculated to be 23.79 %. As shown in Fig. 1d, AFNPs-Zn2+ aggregated and existed as nanoclusters under the influence of hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces (Yu et al., 2022). Compared with AFNPs, the hydrodynamic diameter of AFNPs-Zn2+ increased from 1.33 and 48.85 nm to 74.62 and 280.49 nm (Fig. 1e). The difference in diameter between the two tests can be ascribed to the extension of the polymer chain on the surface of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+, resulting in a larger diameter in the presence of dispersion medium containing solvent than those detected in vacuum (Pandey et al., 2022).

The unique optical property of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ was probed thoroughly. It was observed that AFNPs had photoluminescence properties. The aqueous solution of AFNPs exhibited blue emission under the excitation of 365 nm by a portable UV lamp (Fig. 2 a and S2a-b inset). The fluorescence characteristic of the AFNPs was ascribed to the reorganization of electron-hole pairs in partial sp2-hybridized carbon clusters with crystalline and amorphous structures (Marzariei al., 2013). According to the quinine sulfate method (Fig. S3), the fluorescence quantum yields of the AFNPs synthesized with FB-0, FB-1, and FB-3 were calculated to be 3.07 %, 1.18 %, and 3.97 %, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2a, fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at excitation wavelengths in the range of 300 to 480 nm. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the maximum fluorescence peak gradually red-shifted with increasing emission intensity and then gradually decreased. The excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence properties were attributed to different structures, emission trap sizes, and hybridization components in the carbon clusters (Zheng et al., 2015). In addition, AFNPs synthesized by FB-3 showed strong fluorescence stability, which could maintain relatively constant fluorescence intensity in the near neutral, NaCl, and metal ions environments of Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, and Mn2+ (Fig. S4). Compared with AFNPs, AFNPs-Zn2+ also showed blue fluorescence under a UV lamp (Fig. 2b inset), and demonstrated an excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence property. However, the emission intensity of AFNPs-Zn2+ reduced, which also indicated the formation of AFNPs-Zn2+. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetimes of AFNPs measured by time-dependent single-photon counting were 6.43, 6.29, and 6.39 ns, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2c and S2c-d. However, due to the structural variations of AFNPs, the fluorescence lifetime of AFNPs-Zn2+ decreased to 5.69 ns, which was consistent with the AFNPs extracted from roast beef by Wang et al (Wang et al., 2019). when combined with ferrous ions. In addition, the optical properties of AFNPs were also detected by UV–vis spectra. As shown in Fig. S5a, AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ exhibited obvious absorption peaks (220–350 nm). The peaks at 250 and 270 nm were ascribed to the π-π* transitions of aromatic CC. The acromial peak at 320 nm was derived from the n-π * transition of CO. The slight blue-shift and hyperchromicity in the UV–vis spectra of AFNPs-Zn2+ revealed the formation of AFNPs-Zn2+.

The structural changes of AFNPs were utilized to infer the formation mechanism of AFNPs-Zn2+. In the XRD analysis, depicted in Fig. 2d, the broad peak at 2θ = 23.60° corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, indicating the amorphous structure of AFNPs. In contrast, ZnSO4 showed a typical crystal characterization with many diffraction peaks. Regarding AFNPs-Zn2+, in addition to the broad peaks, there were distinct peaks observed within the 20-40° range, indicating a partially organized structure and confirming the interaction between AFNPs and Zn2+. The functional groups in FB-3, AFNPs, and AFNPs-Zn2+ were confirmed by FTIR spectral analysis (Fig. 2e). Compared to FB-3, the bonds on the surface of AFNPs did not change significantly. The peaks at 3370, 3242, 1612, 1401, and 1095 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of NH, OH, CO, CH, and CO/CN, respectively (Yu et al., 2021). The results showed that the functional groups of FB-3 were retained from fermentation broth to the ultimate composition of AFNPs by hydrothermal synthesis. The disappearance of OH and NH stretching vibration peaks on AFNPs was attributed to the substitution of Zn2+ and AFNPs during the binding process to form O—Zn and N—Zn. Similarly, the disappearance of the two absorption peaks of AFNPs between 1700 and 1600 cm−1 also indicated that CO in the carboxyl group was involved in the binding of AFNPs to Zn2+. The formation of AFNPs-Zn2+ could shift the absorption peak of the CH bond from 1401 to 1425 cm−1. The shift of the absorption peak in AFNPs from 1101 to 1135 cm−1 also implied that CO/CN played an important role in the formation of AFNPs-Zn2+. In addition, the presence of the Zn—O bond at 621 cm−1 was also found in the FTIR spectrum of AFNPs-Zn2+, which also verified the formation of AFNPs-Zn2+ (Wang et al., 2020). In summary, the main groups involved in the binding of AFNPs to Zn2+ were amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups.

The chemical site and elementary composition of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ were further assessed by XPS analysis. The scan survey, as shown in Fig. 2f, reveals the presence of C, N, and O elements in AFNPs with the atomic ratio (Table S1) of 69.31 %, 6.24 %, and 24.85 %, respectively. The elemental composition of AFNPs was determined by the components in FB-3. High-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra in Fig. S6 indicated the presence of various atomic bonds, which was also confirmed by the FTIR results. The peaks in high-resolution C1s spectra at 284.9, 286.1, and 288.1 eV in Fig. S6a revealed the presence of CC, CO, and CO bonds, respectively. The peaks in high-resolution N1s spectra (Fig. S5c) at 398.2 and 399.7 eV also revealed the existence of pyridinic N and NH bonds, respectively. The peaks at 530.9, 531.8, and 532.7 eV in high-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. S5e) were belonging to OH, O—CO, and CO bonds, respectively. The peak at 1019.1 eV in Fig. 2f confirmed the presence of zinc elements in the complex, with a content of 2.25 %. Fig. S5b shows the typical double-peak structure of Zn2p3/2 (1021.9 eV) and Zn2p1/2 (1044.7 eV). The energy segregation between Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2 was about 22.8 eV, indicating the formation of AFNPs-Zn2+ (Li et al., 2019). Furthermore, the peak of the N—Zn (397.9 eV) bond in the high-resolution N1s spectra (Fig. S6d) indicated that the amino group was involved in the interaction between Zn2+ and AFNPs.

Fig. 2g demonstrates the investigation of the electron cloud distribution around the nucleus of the hydrogen atom through 1H NMR spectroscopy, unveiling the interaction pattern between AFNPs and Zn2+. The obvious peak at 2.5 ppm was attributed to DMSO‑d 6. In Fig. 2h, the signal peak of AFNPs at 1.859 ppm moved 0.028 ppm along the direction of the intense magnetic field, which was attributed to the substitution of protons in the methyl and methylene groups of AFNPs by Zn2+ (Zhou et al., 2023). The disappearance of the signal peak at 2.236 ppm in Fig. 2i was attributed to the change of protons in the amino group (Zhou et al., 2023). The signal peak of AFNPs at 3.974 ppm disappeared, and the signal at 4.015 ppm shifted to 4.019 ppm (Fig. 2j), indicating that 1H in the methoxy group was involved in the binding of Zn2+. The displacement of the double-peak signal at 5.441 and 5.457 ppm in Fig. 2k was related to the 1H of the hydroxyl group in the AFNPs (Yu et al., 2022). In addition, the disappearance of the signal peak at 7.175 ppm (Fig. 2l) corresponded to the change of the proton on the aromatic ring. After the formation of AFNPs-Zn2+, the proton signal in the nitrogen-containing heterocycle migrated from 8.474 to 8.233 ppm (Fig. 2m) (Jayanthi et al., 2019). Apparently, the combination between AFNPs and Zn2+ mainly through amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups, which was consistent with the result of FTIR and XPS spectra.

In general, zinc deficiency can cause oxidative stress and DNA damage in the body (Eide, 2011). It is necessary to assess the antioxidant activity of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ to clarify whether AFNPs can be used as Zn2+ carriers to alleviate the histopathological state caused by zinc deficiency. In this work, the antioxidant properties of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ were investigated by measuring the ABTS, DPPH, and hydroxyl radicals scavenging rates and compared with positive control, as shown in Fig. 3 a-f. As shown in Fig. 3a, the ability of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ to scavenge the ABTS radical increased with the increasing concentration. However, the ABTS scavenging ability of AFNPs-Zn2+ decreased slightly after combining with Zn2+. The EC50 value of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ were calculated by polynomial fitting regression to be 133.5 and 140.6 μg/mL, respectively. Meanwhile, the scavenging activity of DPPH radical was also measured. As shown in Fig. 3c, the DPPH radical scavenging activity of AFNPs was further improved during the formation of nanoparticles. The scavenging ability of AFNPs on DPPH radical was concentration-dependent with an EC50 value of 64.9 μg/mL. The EC50 value of AFNPs-Zn2+ in relation to DPPH radical was 72.3 μg/mL. Furthermore, the Fenton reaction with Fe2+ and H2O2 was used to calculate the hydroxyl radical scavenging activity. Fig. 3e indicated that the dose-dependent hydroxyl radical scavenging activity of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+. The results suggested that the scavenging ability of AFNPs on hydroxyl radical reached 57.9 % when the concentration was 0.5 mg/mL. However, the hydroxyl radical scavenging ability of AFNPs-Zn2+ was slightly decreased by the involvement of hydrogen atoms in the reaction, with the EC50 value of 0.51 mg/mL. Nevertheless, AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ still exhibited similar radical scavenging capacity of DPPH and hydroxyl radicals to those of positive controls, which was significantly different from ABTS radicals. These experimental results suggested that AFNPs can be used as zinc nanocarriers to relieve local oxidative damage caused by zinc deficiency.

To better understand the alteration in antioxidant activity, a detailed description of the radical scavenging mechanism of AFNPs-Zn2+ was provided. (Fig. 3g). The scavenging effects of AFNPs-Zn2+ on ABTS, DPPH, and hydroxyl radicals were based on electron donor and proton transfer reactions, respectively. Firstly, the scavenging mechanism of ABTS radicals was an electron transfer reaction. The positively charged ABTS radicals must gain electrons from the antioxidant molecule to form the stable neutral molecule. (Chen et al., 2018) Subsequently, DPPH, as a stable free radical containing nitrogen atom with unpaired electrons and three benzene rings, was trapped by interaction with an antioxidant and incorporation of a hydrogen radical to form a DPPH-H complex (Pyrzynska & Pękal, 2013). In addition, the Fenton reaction with Fe2+ and H2O2 was used to evaluate the capacity of AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ to scavenge hydroxyl radicals. In general, the oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of AFNPs-Zn2+ were considered to be involved in the scavenging of various free radicals (Li et al., 2017). It was found that the electron-doped sites and sp2 carbon could destroy free radicals via adduction and spin precipitation formed by the conjugated carbon skeleton (Cabanero et al., 2017). In fact, the ability of AFNPs-Zn2+ to scavenge radicals might be related to the H-donor behavior of the functional groups at the edge site, including NH, OH, and COOH in combination with free electron delocalization and stabilization at the sp2-hybridized carbon network. However, the decrease in antioxidant capacity after the formation of AFNPs-Zn2+ was related to the participation of electron-donating doped sites and sp2 carbon domains. Nevertheless, AFNPs-Zn2+ still showed excellent antioxidant activity.

Among the critical factors impacting absorption and utilization, the stability of the metal ion complex during digestion is considered to be paramount. Due to the alkalescent environment of the intestine, free zinc could easily form insoluble complex, thus hindering the absorption of zinc in the intestine (Udechukwu, Collins, & Udenigwe, 2016). Herein, the in vitro digestion of AFNPs-Zn2+ was investigated by simulating the oral-gastric-intestinal digestion (Fig. S7a). The results showed that AFNPs-Zn2+ demonstrated various degrees of slight fluorescence quenching after gastrointestinal digestion (Fig. S7b), with quenching efficiencies of 5.42 %, 12.60 %, and 5.21 %, respectively, which were ascribed to the decomposition or inhibition by the digestive fluid. Moreover, the retention of AFNPs-Zn2+ was calculated to assess the stability of AFNPs-Zn2+ after gastrointestinal digestion. As shown in Fig. S7, the retention rate of AFNPs-Zn2+ was only 13.81 % after incubation with simulated gastric fluid (SGF) for 2 h, indicating that the stability of AFNPs-Zn2+ in SGF was barren, resulting in the release of Zn2+. Interestingly, the retention rate of AFNPs-Zn2+ restored to be 40.80 % after digestion with simulated intestinal fluid (SIF) for another 2 h. The variation in the retention of AFNPs-Zn2+ was correlated with the pH environment in the intestine, which induced the self-assembly between AFNPs and Zn2+ (Wang et al., 2020). Fortunately, the in vitro digestion results suggested that AFNPs-Zn2+ can remain stable in gastrointestinal digestion.

The cytocompatibility of AFNPs plays a vital status in determining the applicability of nanoparticles as Zn2+ nanocarriers. As shown in Fig. 4 a, AFNPs solutions (0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 1, and 2 mg/mL) were incubated with IEC-6 cells, and the cell viability was measured to exceed 100 %. The results showed that AFNPs were reliable enough to be used as zinc nanocarriers, because they were prepared by non-toxic raw materials and eco-friendly methods. In addition, the cytotoxicity of AFNPs-Zn2+ was compared with common zinc supplements (ZnSO4 and zinc gluconate) at the same zinc ion concentrations. Fig. 4b shows the cytotoxicity decrease in the order of ZnSO4 > zinc gluconate > AFNPs-Zn2+. Exhilaratingly, AFNPs-Zn2+ had no obvious toxic effects on IEC-6 cells in the range of 0–20 μg/mL. As for the zinc gluconate and ZnSO4, the cell viability dropped below 70 % when zinc concentrations were increased to 20 μg/mL. The blood compatibility was also the key to the effective application of zinc supplements. Hemolysis also occurred when the toxic substance interacted with blood cells. Fortunately, AFNPs and AFNPs-Zn2+ demonstrated no significant hemolysis phenomenon with a lower hemolysis rate (5 %) when incubated with blood cells for 1 h or 3 h (Fig. 4c). Collectively, AFNPs-Zn2+ had good biocompatibility, which had the potential to be a nutritional zinc supplement.

It was well known that zinc deficiency was a frequent condition in human populations, which can affect cell function by inducing oxidative stress, thereby leading to disease (Oteiza & Mackenzie, 2005). In this research, N, N, N′, N′-Tetrakis (2-pyridylmethyl) ethylenediamine (TPEN), an intracellular zinc chelator, was used to construct a zinc-deficient IEC-6 cells model. The IEC-6 cells were stained by calcein-AM and PI after TPEN intervention to observe living/dead cells. In living cells, intracellular esterase was able to transform calcein-AM to calcein, generating obvious green fluorescence. PI can penetrate the cytomembrane of dead cells and combine with DNA, generating red fluorescence (Wang et al., 2017). As shown in Fig. 4d-f, the treatment of TPEN resulted in a drastic decline in living cells labeled with calcein (green) and an increase in the dead cells labeled with PI (red) in comparison to the control group. Simultaneously, significant variations, such as shrinking and rounding, were also observed in dead cells. Fortunately, AFNPs-Zn2+ could alleviate the death of zinc-deficient cells caused by TPEN, and the effect was more pronounced in the high-dose (80 μg/mL) group. The determination of cell viability by MTT assay also showed the same results in Fig. 4g. AFNPs-Zn2+ could alleviate the decrease in cell viability caused by TPEN. After incubation with AFNPs-Zn2+ (80 μg/mL), the cell viability recovered from 45.1 % to 90.3 %. Collectively, all these results suggested that AFNPs-Zn2+ could relieve the cell damage caused by zinc deficiency.

In addition, DCFH-DA was applied to evaluate ROS level, and the intensity of green fluorescence was positively correlated with the content of ROS. As shown in Fig. 5 a and b, in comparison to the control group, the green fluorescence caused by ROS was easily examined to increase by 175.5 % after TPEN stimulation. After incubation with AFNPs-Zn2+, the fluorescence intensity decreased obviously. As the concentration of AFNPs-Zn2+ increased, the generation of ROS decreased. After treatment with 80 μg/mL AFNPs-Zn2+, the intensity of green fluorescence was similar to the control group with no significant difference. The results indicated that AFNPs-Zn2+ could reduce the elevation of ROS levels caused by TPEN, thereby alleviating oxidative stress. Synchronously, the activity of various antioxidant enzymes in damaged IEC-6 cells was used to reflect the protective effect of AFNPs-Zn2+ against cellular zinc deficiency. As shown in Fig. 5c-h, the levels of T-AOC, CAT, SOD, and GSH were obviously decreased, while the levels of MDA and LDH were markedly increased in the TPEN-treated groups. After the cells were incubated with AFNPs-Zn2+ for 24 h, the levels of T-AOC, CAT, SOD, and GSH significantly increased. The levels of MDA and LDH significantly reduced compared with the TPEN-treated group. Collectively, all these results suggested that the AFNPs-Zn2+ had superior antioxidation capacity and zinc supplementation effect, which could effectively alleviate the damage caused by zinc deficiency in vitro.

The distribution of AFNPs-Zn2+ in various organs was investigated after gavage for 0, 0.5, 2, 6, and 24 h, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6 a. Fluorescence signals were observed in the stomach, intestine, kidney, and testis after the gavage of AFNPs-Zn2+ (Fig. 6b) compared with the control mice after the gavage of distilled water. These results illustrated that AFNPs-Zn2+ had strong permeability, and could be transported to mice organs through body fluids (including blood and lymph) after being taken by mice, which was attributed to its ultra-small size (Zhang et al., 2021). It was found the fluorescence intensity in the stomach, intestine, and kidney (Fig. 6c) of mice increased first and then decreased with the accumulation of digestive time bycomputing the mean intensity of the fluorescent signal area. As shown in Fig. 6d, the variation of zinc content in the above organs was consistent with the variation in fluorescence intensity. After oral administration of AFNPs-Zn2+ for 6 h, a strong fluorescence signal and zinc content could still be detected in the intestine. It indicated that AFNPs-Zn2+ could be retained in the intestine for a long time, which was more favorable for AFNPs-Zn2+ to exert its zinc supplementation effect in vivo. It was worth noting that the fluorescence intensity in the testis gradually increased with digestion time. Studies have demonstrated the abundance of zinc in the testes of male animals, highlighting its involvement in the synthesis and maturation of spermatozoa (Lu et al., 2015) The accumulation of zinc in mice testis also suggested that AFNPs-Zn2+ had the potential to act as zinc supplement to the reproductive system of mice. This work reaffirmed the significant importance of bioactive compounds across various fields, as evidenced by numerous previously published papers (Abdel-Raheem et al., 2022; Fouad et al., 2023; Zaki et al., 2018).

In summary, AFNPs prepared by green synthesis method using A. auricula fermentation broth were used as nanocarriers for Zn2+ delivery. Among them, AFNPs synthesized with three strains fermentation broth (FB-3) showed excellent properties, including nano-scale size, outstanding fluorescence stability, praised antioxidant activity, and superior zinc-binding capability. The functional groups (e.g., amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups) on the surface of the AFNPs made an important contribution to the Zn2+ binding. The AFNPs-Zn2+ not only maintained great stability in gastrointestinal digestion, but also had superior biocompatibility. The AFNPs-Zn2+ exhibited not only excellent stability during gastrointestinal digestion but also demonstrated superior biocompatibility. Synchronously, AFNPs-Zn2+ could alleviate cell damage caused by zinc deficiency. Moreover, the AFNPs-Zn2+ had no obvious acute toxicity after oral administration, and were distributed in the stomach, intestine, kidney, and testis. Our data suggested that the AFNPs synthesized with A. auricula fermentation broth might be used as potential Zn2+ nanocarriers to alleviate the damage caused by zinc deficiency. However, the zinc supplementation effect of AFNPs-Zn2+ in vivo needs to be verified by further animal experiments.

Xiaoting Yu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Shanghua Xing: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Mingqian Tan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
